sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: All 8 clickable cells are green, but the level is not complete. The center cell (dark gray, size=208) appears to be part of the background. I need to find what else to interact with. Maybe the target pattern panel, the blue bar, or keyboard navigation.
- Goal model: Click all 4 white blocks in the bottom-right grid to turn them green, matching the cross pattern shown in the bottom-left panel.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT for navigation, MOUSE for clicking. Need to test what they do.
- Recent findings: Grid is fully green. The top track blue indicator has been shrinking throughout the game.
- Open questions: What's the next task? Maybe click the remaining gray blocks (corners and center) that don't have purple dots?
- Plan: Try clicking other elements on the board - the target pattern panel or the blue bar. Also try keyboard navigation.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 42 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at step 42, nothing changed after clicking the target pattern panel at (55, 17). The grid still shows 8 green cells and 1 gray center cell. The level is still not complete.
Let me reconsider the game. Looking at the segmentation:
- id=8: color=G (dark gray), size=208, children=[9, 10, 11, 18, 19, 20, 22, 23, 24] - this is the grid background containing 9 child cells
- id=9-11, 18-20, 22-24: These are the 9 cells in the grid
- id=12: color=G (dark gray), size=36, children=[13, 14, 16, 17, 21] - this is the target pattern panel
- id=13: color=g (gray), size=48 - background of target panel
- id=14, 16, 17: color=p (purple), size=4 each - the 4 purple dots in the target pattern
- id=21: Also a child of id=12
Wait, the target pattern panel has 4 purple dots (id=14, 16, 17, and maybe 21 is also purple). Let me check what id=21 is.
Looking at the grid cells:
- id=9, 10, 11: color=N (light green), size=9 each - these are 3 green cells
- id=18, 19, 20: color=N/g, size=9 each - these are more cells
- id=22, 23, 24: More cells
The grid has 9 cells total (3x3). 8 of them are green (N), and 1 is gray (g). The center cell is the gray one.
Wait, but I clicked the center cell earlier and it didn't change. Let me look at the image again. In step 42, the center cell is still gray. But the segmentation shows id=19 as color=g (gray), size=9. So the center cell IS a separate clickable cell, not part of the background.
But when I clicked (55, 30) earlier, nothing changed. Maybe I need to click it again, or maybe there's a different way to interact with it?
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. The target pattern shows 4 purple dots in a cross shape. The grid has 9 cells. Maybe the game is about clicking cells to match the pattern, but I've been clicking the wrong cells.
Wait, I just realized something. The target pattern shows 4 purple dots in a cross shape (top-center, middle-left, middle-right, bottom-center). But the grid has 8 green cells now, which includes the corners. So I've clicked too many cells!
Maybe the game is about clicking ONLY the cells that match the target pattern (the 4 cross cells), not all 8 cells. But I already clicked all 8 cells, and they're all green. Maybe I need to "unclick" the corner cells to make them gray again?
Or maybe the game has a different mechanic - like clicking cells toggles them between green and gray, and I need to match the exact pattern?
Let me try clicking one of the corner cells to see if it toggles back to gray.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the target pattern panel had no effect. The grid still has 8 green cells and 1 gray center cell. The level is not complete. I need to reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe clicking cells toggles them, and I need to match the exact target pattern (4 cross cells only, not corners).
Plan: Try clicking a corner cell to see if it toggles back to gray, which would confirm the toggle mechanic.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check the center cell in the grid
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find grid cells (children of id=8)
grid_cell_ids = [9, 10, 11, 18, 19, 20, 22, 23, 24]
print("Grid cells:")
for nid in grid_cell_ids:
    for n in nodes:
        if n['id'] == nid:
            print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} size={n['pixels']}")
            break

# Try clicking a corner cell to see if it toggles
# Top-left corner is around (50, 25)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 50, 'col': 25}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  id=20 color=N size=9

  id=23 color=N size=9
  id=24 color=N size=9
